Modules
Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → LLD foundations and prompt framing → Responsibilities, interfaces, and seams

Lesson 2 of 3

Responsibilities, interfaces, and seams

Assign behavior to the right object, define collaboration boundaries, and keep business rules testable without framework noise.

25-35 min LLD flow LLD foundations and prompt framing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Interviewers look for designs where responsibilities are obvious, dependencies are controlled, and logic remains testable as the prompt evolves.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Assign ownership deliberately Keep orchestration separate from entity state2. Design collaboration seams Inject dependencies that have external side effects3. Keep the API readable Choose method names that match domain actions

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Interviewers look for designs where responsibilities are obvious, dependencies are controlled, and logic remains testable as the prompt evolves. 2. Assign ownership deliberately Keep orchestration separate from entity state3. Design collaboration seams Inject dependencies that have external side effects4. Keep the API readable Choose method names that match domain actions
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Interviewers look for designs where responsibilities are obvious, dependencies are controlled, and logic remains testable as the prompt evolves.

What to say first

  • Explain why each core class exists.
  • Name the interface boundaries that isolate external dependencies.
  • Keep the public API aligned with domain language.
2

Study section

Assign ownership deliberately

Each class should own a focused set of rules or state transitions. If multiple objects need the same rule, introduce a shared policy or service instead of duplicating logic.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Keep orchestration separate from entity state
  • Prefer behavior-rich models over anemic data bags when rules matter
  • Move cross-cutting concerns behind interfaces
3

Study section

Design collaboration seams

Create interfaces where the dependency is likely to change independently, such as persistence, notification delivery, pricing strategies, or clock access.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Inject dependencies that have external side effects
  • Model the minimum interface required by the caller
  • Use seams to improve testability, not to satisfy a pattern checklist
4

Study section

Keep the API readable

Method names, parameter objects, and return types should make legal usage clear and invalid usage hard.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Choose method names that match domain actions
  • Use small value objects for grouped parameters when it improves clarity
  • Return explicit results or errors when failure is part of the workflow

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain why each core class exists.
  • Name the interface boundaries that isolate external dependencies.
  • Keep the public API aligned with domain language.
  • Show how the design stays unit-testable.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Creating interfaces for every class even when no alternate implementation exists.
  • Putting validation, persistence, and workflow orchestration in a single god object.
  • Using generic method names like process or handle when the domain action is specific.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When should a rule live inside an entity versus a service?
  • How do you decide whether a dependency deserves an interface?
  • What makes an API feel interview-ready rather than framework-driven?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

API design: REST, RPC, and contracts Application architecture and APIs

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Responsibilities, interfaces, and seams" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • When should a rule live inside an entity versus a service?
  • Assign ownership deliberately
  • Design collaboration seams
  • Explain why each core class exists.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
